The only point we should discuss is whether we want to enable this endpoint by default, or have the user explicitly enable it (e.g., `ENABLE_DIAGNOSTICS`, similar to what we're doing with `ENABLE_CONFIG_UPDATES`). We do strongly recommend our users to always only bind the edge port to 127.0.0.1, but that may not always be the case, unfortunately.

I guess the main issue is that we'd want to enable the diagnostics for an already running instance - because it may be hard to re-create a faulty state, and we don't want to make it a requirement to re-start the container (to enable the environment config). One option would be to allow the user to `docker exec localstack_main touch /tmp/.enable-diagnostics`, which could then override the `ENABLE_DIAGNOSTICS` environment variable.

Don't want to over-engineer the solution, but security could definitely become a concern here, and we should make a conscious decision.

A couple high level points:
1. I think we should limit the amount we make modifications to the superclass Trainer inside the subclass Trainer, and try to fit in with the superclass APIs as much as possible. HFTrainer will serve as the primary example for how we integrate other Trainers in the future, so it would be great to leverage the APIs that we have built.
2. I also think we should limit the amount we are modifying Huggingface behavior and try to use their public APIs as much as possible, leave validation & error handling to them, and fail quickly if the user tries to do things that we don't support instead of overriding this behavior to get this to work. This will make sure our implementation is easy to maintain and is future-proof (after all they are working on transformers v5 and a lot of their APIs will be changing). This was a mistake I made with ray-lightning, and it's been a pain to maintain it, so hoping not to repeat this again! Good thing is, if their APIs are not flexible enough to support our use cases, we can work with the HF team to make the necessary changes.

There are going to be errors around `clients_first_seen` dealing with the `main` ping while the enrollment event is in the `events` ping, so this might exclude a fraction of new users who enroll on day 1.

It might be worth checking this segment against e.g. an onboarding experiment, to see what fraction of new users we're missing. If it's too high, we might want to mitigate the situation by setting `window_start=-1`. `mozanalysis` won't let us set `window_end=1` because it could introduce bias; if the data shows a strong need to change this for this segment then we should do so.

I think that the segment as expressed in this PR is likely to be useful regardless of the outcome of the above checks that I recommend, so I don't think they should necessarily block merging this.

I'm triaging some PRs pending a release and see that there are review requests in https://github.com/jupyterhub/nbgitpuller/pull/291#issuecomment-1440563517 not yet implemented.

---

My take is that this can be a useful feature, but I agree with Yuvi that this must be very secure to be accepted.

I figure nbgitpuller is not jupyterhub aware, only the link generator is. So how could the jupyterhub admin disable this by default or similar? Also if the jupyterhub admin would enable this, how would users know its enabled - are we relying on the jupyterhub admin to successfully communicate this to all users? 

I think I lean towards not wanting to accept a feature like this unless the individual user that has clicked the link and pulled content is prompted to do something after pulling. The complexity of supporting that may not be something I think is in scope for nbgitpuller as a project, depending on how its proposed to be accomplished.

This seems like a huge amount of work, so thanks for that, @drmoose!

However, this is also quite a bit of code, and I'm not entirely convinced it should be added to pybind11. But please do try to convince me (and others), @drmoose and @bstaletic.

A few questions that first come to mind, when seeing this:
- `sys.argv` can be set directly, I believe (through `py::module::import("sys").attr("argv") = ...`). What's the reason to call `PySys_SetArgvEx`?
- Why do we want to support the `char **argv, int argc` signature, and not just `std::vector<const char*>` or even `std::vector<std::string>`. I can see that C/C++'s `main` takes these arguments, of course, but wouldn't it almost always be bad practice to immediately pass on these arguments of `main` to Python? Wouldn't you, in a situation where Python is embedded, expect the main, embedding program to handle the arguments before deciding to pass on a few to Python? Or maybe the embedding program wants to just set a fixed set of arguments? If not, it's seems more like "wrapping" CPython, rather than embedding it. It's also kind of annoying to produce a `char**`, when you're not getting it as `main` parameters (but from `char**` to `std::vector<std::string>` is reasonably straightforward).
- This argument is further strengthened by the `updatepath` in `PySys_SetArgvEx`, which tries out whether `argv[0]` is a script file (which, if you're passing on `main`s arguments, is wrong, because it'll be the executable's name?). This also somehow doesn't sense to me, except when you're thinly "wrapping" Python rather than embedding it in a larger program (and even then, not really, because it's the executable). The question here is what the default assumption should be; I'd say pybind11 should assume as little as possible, here, as long as it's possible for the user to achieve this if necessary.
- Why is `Py_DecodeLocale` being used; what advantage does it offer? I'd say it's up to the embedding program to decide on the encoding, rather than use Python's defaults here? Shouldn't we stick to pybind11's ["utf-8 everywhere"](https://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/advanced/cast/strings.html#passing-python-strings-to-c)?
- Do we really want to set `sys.argv == ['']` by default? I'm actually fine with that, but this was once rejected by @wjakob (https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/issues/1768#issuecomment-503474136). See also discussions in #1768 and #2061.
- On a related not: does this need to be part of the constructor? (For some reason, the order of the arguments seems funky to me, thought I know it's to keep backwards compatibility with the API without these arguments, ofc). Couldn't this be a method?

@ArturNiederfahrenhorst I just did a more thorough pass on your LSTM tests. The test is not really testing the rollout of LSTM cells during training (test_forward_train)

What you are doing right now:
- stacking obs, actions, and states in multiple BATCHES. Where is the time dimension then?
- This is like using LSTM cells as a fully connected cell :) 

What you should be doing:
- stack obs, and actions in both batches and times. So that the final shape of batch is (b, t, ho) and the final shape of actions is (b, t, ha)
- stack the state_in to be of shape (b, n_layer=1, hs) 
- if the number of steps are not compatible with (b, t) reshaping you need to zero pad. For example if you took 30 steps and your max_rollout_len = 20. You need to create a (1, 20, ho) obs tensor and concat if with a (1, 10, ho) obs. You cannot. So you need to zero pad the second one to make it (1, 20, ho) and take care of zeros with masking. In this example your state_in shape should be (2, 1, hs). 

Makes sense?
I would recommend taking out the lstm stuff in forward_train from your test. your rollout test seems correct.

I think we can do this, but roll it back if it doesn't really help.

I think what we decided is:
* Worker Runner sometimes thinks a worker isn't active when it is
* Worker Runner calls Worker Manager and tells it to remove the worker
* Worker Runner triggers a shutdown of the machine
* Possibly something causes the worker to restart, so it calls claimWork again, and is given a task
* Worker dies before task is resolved, so is eventually resolved exception/claim-expired by some Queue process

We decided this patch may be useful for reducing the cases of this happening, but the ideal fix would be to fix worker runner from falsely thinking the worker is running, or perhaps docker-worker is really hanging, but then when worker manager shuts down the worker, docker-worker is starting up again for some reason. However, since we are discontinuing docker-worker, and possibly worker-runner too, this is a reasonable fix to try, to see if it improves the situation.

If after this, things are no better, we will roll this back.
If this fixes the issue only for docker-worker, and the needle doesn't change much on generic-worker, we will also roll this back once we have removed docker-worker from taskcluster.
If this fixes things also with worker-runner/generic-worker, we can try removing worker runner to see if things improve. If there is no worker-runner, and there is no docker-worker, and this fix continues to improve the situation, we need to revisit the design to understand what is going wrong. In short, this fix shouldn't really be needed, it is masking a problem which is occurring elsewhere, and we are only making this fix this way for now, since we are planning on getting rid of docker-worker and possible worker-runner too.

> I also needed a different interface to the Credentials API, since I wanted to control the encoding when calling `CredWrite` and also add the JSON attribute. So I added a `cffi`-based interface directly to `Windows.py`. This is ugly, but I'm not adept at setuptools and wasn't sure how to properly add a new file to the system. I also don't know if I updated `setup.cfg` correcly to reflect the new dependency on `cffi`. I think it is more maintainable to have the code under `keyring`'s roof, rather than relying on the Enthought package.

I'd like to revisit this consideration. While I do like the idea of not being dependent on another implementation, it means keyring is essentially creating its own re-implementation of the Enthought package and adopting the burden that goes along with that (separate maintenance burden, diverging implementations, loss of re-use).

If we step back, I'd like to capture an issue for why pywin32-ctypes is inadequate (probably upstream in that project) and consider solving that issue first before chosing to fork a new implementation. I do think it's plausible that the upstream implementation isn't flexible enough for keyring's needs due to its legacy association with pywin32 and the interfaces it provided. I would like to shed _that_ legacy.

If we decide to adopt a unique implementation, I'd like to consider whether cffi is the best approach. Adding a dependency on cffi adds a _binary_ dependency for keyring, meaning anyone needing keyring cannot install from source without having a build environment set up. Historically, that was a much bigger problem before the advent of eggs/wheels and their common adoption in PyPI. This issue was recently [reported in twine](https://github.com/pypa/twine/issues/837#issuecomment-979275593) when users of Alpine Linux were required to install keyring and by extension cffi, but could not.

Finally, while I believe the sharding implementation here is sound enough (and provably so with the proposed additional tests), I'd like to consider a refactoring that would separate the concerns (sharded vs. unsharded). In particular, I imagine a `SimpleKeyring` that implements `WinVaultKeyring` as currrently implemented and then a specialized subclass `ShardedKeyring` that implements the sharding behavior. Even if the `SimpleKeyring` is disabled (priority -1), it would provide a means to easily test without the sharding behavior and also make it clearer what functionality is pertinent to that concern. That separation of concerns is apparent in this diff, but it's going to be much harder to reason about going forward.

To summarize, here's how I'd like to move forward:

1. Determine if pywin32-ctypes can support the necessary interfaces herein and at least capture any shortcomings.
2. Port the existing backend to an API that supports the necessary interfaces (perhaps an updated version of pywin32-ctypes or maybe a bespoke implementation). Cut a release to get early feedback on any regressions related to that change.
3. Implement the ShardedKeyring as a specialized subclass or mix-in, encapsulating the sharding logic in one class.

This incremental approach, while it may take longer, should give us better visibility into the effects of different changes.

A few comments:
- We do know that accuracy is not affected but it might be the case that a kernel is broken/buggy on setups we haven't tested (floats for instance) or that the specific augmentation doesn't have much effect on accuracy. So as you highlighted we must check things very thoroughly to understand if this is a test problem or an actual bug.
- I wonder if it's worth it to focus on single pixel differences rather than averages. As averages can accumulate, it might worth examining if max single pixel tolerances can be stricter.
- Some of the tolerances look scary. We should bisect and revert any speed optimizations that cause them. It might be also worth checking our tensor kernels against PIL. Stable tests do that now and this is how we ensure that stable and PIL stay aligned. V2 doesn't have such tests (of course it compares itself with V1) but due to the increased tolerances we might be missing something important.

Another round of benchmarks after the new commits. Benchmark removed CUDA test and only tested one thread. On the flip side, measurements are now running longer to reduce the noise.
benchmark script

```python import pathlib import pickle import torch from torch.utils import benchmark import functools from torchvision.prototype.transforms import functional as F description = "PR" # "main", "PR" def make_inputs(*, input_dtype, output_dtype, shape=(3, 512, 512)): if input_dtype.is_floating_point: image = torch.rand(shape, dtype=input_dtype) else: image = torch.randint(0, torch.iinfo(input_dtype).max + 1, shape, dtype=input_dtype) return image, output_dtype sub_labels_and_input_fns = [ ("float to float", functools.partial(make_inputs, input_dtype=torch.float32, output_dtype=torch.float64)), ("float to int", functools.partial(make_inputs, input_dtype=torch.float32, output_dtype=torch.uint8)), (" int to float", functools.partial(make_inputs, input_dtype=torch.uint8, output_dtype=torch.float32)), (" int to int (down)", functools.partial(make_inputs, input_dtype=torch.int32, output_dtype=torch.uint8)), (" int to int (up)", functools.partial(make_inputs, input_dtype=torch.uint8, output_dtype=torch.int32)), ] timers = [ benchmark.Timer( stmt="convert_image_dtype(*inputs)", globals=dict( convert_image_dtype=F.convert_image_dtype, inputs=inputs_fn(), ), label="convert_image_dtype perf improvements", sub_label=sub_label, description=description, num_threads=1, ) for sub_label, inputs_fn in sub_labels_and_input_fns ] measurements = [timer.blocked_autorange(min_run_time=15) for timer in timers] with open(f"{description}.measurements", "wb") as fh: pickle.dump(measurements, fh) measurements = [] for file in pathlib.Path(".").glob("*.measurements"): with open(file, "rb") as fh: measurements.extend(pickle.load(fh)) comparison = benchmark.Compare(measurements) comparison.trim_significant_figures() comparison.print() ```

``` [- convert_image_dtype perf improvements --] | main | PR 1 threads: --------------------------------- float to float | 90 | 83 float to int | 380 | 380 int to float | 138 | 134 int to int (down) | 1100 | 402 int to int (up) | 127 | 92 Times are in microseconds (us). ``` - "float to float", "int to float", "int to int (up)" did not change from the last benchmarks and are still faster - "int to int (down)" now uses bit shifts and is 3x faster

My main question regarding the target_id and class_id is whether we need to distinguish them. Alternatively, is it general enough? I find it helpful to think about multi-task where the task is undefined.

For example, XGBoost is working toward quantile regression with confident interval support. This leads to a potential future where we can train a model with a multi-quantile-multi-target model. In such cases, how do we distinguish the task boundary?

I'm not sure this is what we want per default.

For instance, with this change, if a Tune run is stopped and I restore and want to continue it, my training will completely fail and it will be impossible for me to resume at all, as the wandb IDs already exist and I can't override the stored setting.

I think this setting is trying to solve a problem for the wrong reasons. The original issue was that duplicate trial IDs were generated when starting parallel new experiments, leading to conflicts. We've since improved the trial generation (#29900) so the problem shouldn't come up anymore. And if it did, the correct recommendation here should be that the user should just pass `resume="never"` to their wandb config.


Let's consider the journeys and requirements real quick.

1. I start a run. I expect each run to be logged separately to wandb.

This generally worked and was improved by #29900. For additional guarantees, users can choose to pass `resume="never"`.

2. I start and stop a run. I resume the run. I expect the resumed run to be logged to wandb.

This works at the moment because we just overwrite existing runs per default. With the current PR, this wouldn't work anymore, users would have to pass `resume=None` manually. But this is a far more common case than the problem we encountered in 1.

3. I start and stop a run. I resume the run. I expect the resumed run to be logged to wandb but _not to lose previously logged data_.

This is a bit more tricky, as we may resume from an earlier point in time, so the results in wandb could look a bit odd. I think it may be worthwhile exploring this option. However, so far we haven't seen any user requests for this.

To look into this, we should probably try it out ourselves - how do the results look like with stopping/resuming at the moment (and overwriting) - does this only overwrite local results or also online results? What is the difference if we stop/resume with `resume="auto"`?

Generally I think the current default is sensible as it covers the most common use cases gracefully and we didn't run into problems with the resume behavior so far (other than what we fixed in #29900). But very open to discussions here.

Nice, 👍 really useful debug output - this will be super helpful in diagnosing user issues more efficiently. 🎉  

The only point we should discuss is whether we want to enable this endpoint by default, or have the user explicitly enable it (e.g., `ENABLE_DIAGNOSTICS`, similar to what we're doing with `ENABLE_CONFIG_UPDATES`). We do strongly recommend our users to always only bind the edge port to 127.0.0.1, but that may not always be the case, unfortunately.

I guess the main issue is that we'd want to enable the diagnostics for an already running instance - because it may be hard to re-create a faulty state, and we don't want to make it a requirement to re-start the container (to enable the environment config). One option would be to allow the user to `docker exec localstack_main touch /tmp/.enable-diagnostics`, which could then override the `ENABLE_DIAGNOSTICS` environment variable.

Don't want to over-engineer the solution here, but security could definitely become a concern here, and we should make a conscious decision.
